submission_document.text_summary:
Difficulty: ✅ MEDIUM

Status: ✅ Solvable (all tests passed by at least one agent run)

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-6: 100.0% (5/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 40.0% (2/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 3 other

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_subsystem_unit[./graph]: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_subsystem_unit[./flow]: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_subsystem_unit[./wave]: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_subsystem_unit[./prior]: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p01: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p02: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p03: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p04: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p05: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p06: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p07: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p08: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p09: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p10: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_p11: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h01: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h02: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h03: 7 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h04: 7 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h05: 7 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h06: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h07: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h08: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h09: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h10: 7 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ✅ PASS, ## Job Summary: Go Simulator Repair Task

### 1. Overall Results
**0/3 trials passed** (all received reward 0.0). No agent successfully completed the task. All three trials used the same general approach pattern, suggesting a single model/agent configuration was tested.

---

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

All three trials converged on the **same root failure**: after correctly fixing the four operator stubs, agents over-engineered a fix to `sim/driver.go` that broke congestion modeling for fixture packs.

| Phase | Result |
|-------|--------|
| Fix 4 operator stubs (op_path, op_split, op_rank, op_phase) | ✅ All 3 agents succeeded |
| Pass unit tests | ✅ All 3 agents passed subsystem tests |
| Fix peak_ratio/jam_band computation | ❌ All 3 agents failed |

**The exact failure mode was identical across all trials**: agents introduced a "time-spreading" or "cumulative division" model in `driver.go` to bring `peak_ratio` down for the bundled pack, but this over-smoothed congestion — causing fixture packs explicitly designed to show congestion (`dual_bottleneck`, `shelter_tight`, `priority_stress`) to incorrectly report `jam_band="clear"`. Test failure counts were 6/25 (tbench-task__4h5rPQc), 7/25 (tbench-task__qP5aY9M), and 5/25 (tbench-task__ozTTDc8).

**Secondary failure** in tbench-task__qP5aY9M only: `late_hazard` fixture failed because the agent did not implement hazard-avoiding route selection — convoys were still routed through the blocked bridge edge.

---

### 3. Hack Check
**No cheating detected.** All three trials passed `reward_hacking` checks. Agents worked through legitimate code edits, shell commands, and binary rebuilds. No access to `solution/` directories, no writes to `reward.txt`, no manipulation of test files.

---

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues

**No instruction defects** — all three trials passed `task_specification` checks. The spec was clear:
- `plan-schema.md` explicitly defines `peak_ratio` as **maximum per-tick edge utilization** (not cumulative)
- Jam band thresholds (`clear < 0.65`, `tight < peak_cap`, else `severe`) were documented

The failures were agent reasoning errors: agents saw `peak_ratio=1.0` or `0.821` on the bundled pack and incorrectly concluded the load model needed redesigning, rather than recognizing the operators alone were the source of the bug. A minimal operator-only fix would have produced correct fixture outputs without touching `driver.go`.

---

### 5. Progress on Failed Trials

Agents were **consistently ~76–80% complete**:
- 19/25 tests passed (tbench-task__4h5rPQc)
- 18/25 tests passed (tbench-task__qP5aY9M)  
- 20/25 tests passed (tbench-task__ozTTDc8)

The gap was entirely concentrated in congestion-sensitive fixture tests. The hardest sub-task — recognizing that `driver.go` should be left alone after fixing the operators — was missed every time.

---

### 6. Key Differences Between Agents

All three trials appear to be the same model configuration. Differences were marginal:
- **tbench-task__ozTTDc8** performed best (20/25), failing only 5 tests
- **tbench-task__qP5aY9M** performed worst (18/25) and was the only trial with an additional unique failure (hazard routing)
- **tbench-task__4h5rPQc** was the only trial to explicitly note the `all-or-nothing` grading insight, suggesting slightly better task meta-awareness

**The core fix needed** — computing peak utilization as a per-tick maximum rather than a cumulative average — was misidentified by all agents as a load-spreading problem rather than a measurement problem.
submission_document.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
❌ fail - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction covers the bundled-pack pass conditions and the fixture file names but leaves several hardened-test behaviors undescribed. test_h03–h06 expect specific jam_band values (tight/severe) and numeric peak_ratio ranges for each fixture (e.g., test_h06 requires 0.95 ≤ peak_ratio ≤ 0.99 for priority_stress). test_h07 requires determinism across back-to-back runs, which is never mentioned. test_h10 manipulates phase_count=1 and expects jam_band='severe' with peak_ratio≥1.0, a behavior not described anywhere in instruction.md or plan-schema.md. An agent reading only instruction.md would not know these properties are graded.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Every behavior the instruction explicitly describes is covered by at least one test: binary presence (plan fixture), bundled-pack field values (test_p01–p06), exact key set (test_p11), fixture replays (test_p07–p10, test_h03–h06), unit-test passage for all four subsystem packages (test_subsystem_unit), and field recomputation rather than copying (test_h08/h09).
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: The test file is well-organised: helper functions are clearly named (run_planner, assert_jam_peak_coherent, assert_bundled_completion), every test function has a one-sentence docstring explaining what it checks, and test IDs follow a two-tier scheme (test_p* for public/basic checks, test_h* for hardened/edge-case checks), making the intent of each test immediately clear.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile copies only source packages plus data into the image; neither tests/ nor solution/ is included. Expected output values are entirely derived at runtime from the simulation execution. The schema_tag is read dynamically from labels.go rather than hardcoded in the test. The decoy/ package contains plausible-looking but unused functions (LaneHint, SpreadScore) that could distract an agent looking for shortcuts. No ground-truth output files are planted in the image.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: plan-schema.md (referenced explicitly in instruction.md) documents all six output fields with their JSON keys, Go types, and exact band thresholds (clear < 0.65, tight < peak_cap, severe ≥ peak_cap). The schema is normative and complete, not merely illustrative.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base image is pinned by SHA-256 digest (golang:1.22-bookworm@sha256:…). All apt packages carry explicit version strings (ca-certificates=20230311+deb12u1, curl=7.88.1-10+deb12u14, python3=3.11.2-1+b1, tmux=3.3a-3). uv is installed at version 0.9.5 via a versioned URL. Python packages are pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). No unversioned external dependencies are present.
✅ pass - typos: All file paths in instruction.md (/app/bin/evac_planner, /app/data/evac_pack.json, /app/output/evac_plan.json, /app/docs/plan-schema.md, fixture filenames) match the actual files in the environment. Variable names, package imports, and JSON field names are consistent across source, schema docs, and tests. No typos were found.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile contains no COPY instruction for tests/ or solution/. The verifier runs from /tests/ which is mounted at grading time, separate from the build image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh implements corrected algorithms for op_path.go (k-shortest paths via edge deletion), op_split.go (capacity-weighted flow distribution), op_phase.go (modular phase assignment), and op_rank.go (vulnerability-ratio ordering), then rebuilds the binary with go build and runs it. The answer is derived entirely through computation, not by echoing a pre-computed result.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: instruction.md explicitly names the output file: 'writes /app/output/evac_plan.json'. The tests confirm this path as PLAN = APP / 'output' / 'evac_plan.json', matching the instruction.

submission_document.test_review:
================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task presents a broken Go-based city evacuation simulator where the agent
must diagnose and repair four subsystem packages (graph routing, flow
splitting, wave phasing, and priority ranking) so that the planner binary
produces correct evacuation plans. The solution fixes algorithmic bugs in
op_path.go (adds k-shortest-path via edge removal), op_split.go (capacity-
weighted distribution), op_phase.go (modulo staggering), and op_rank.go
(vulnerability-ratio sorting). The test suite contains 23+ test cases
validating subsystem unit tests, bundled pack output, fixture generalization,
mutation robustness, and determinism.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile Instead of test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 21-23)
Problem: pytest==8.4.1 and pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5 are installed in the Docker
         image rather than in test.sh. The standard pattern installs test
         dependencies in test.sh to keep the agent environment clean and make
         test.sh self-contained.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \               │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-venv --seed \                  │
│      && /opt/tbench-venv/bin/pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.  │
│  3.5                                                                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Move pytest installation into test.sh and remove the venv
setup from the Dockerfile:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # In test.sh, before running pytest:                                       │
│  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh                     │
│  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                │
│  uv venv .tbench-testing                                                    │
│  source .tbench-testing/bin/activate                                        │
│  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Installing test-only dependencies in the Dockerfile makes test.sh
dependent on the image build and deviates from the standard self-contained
test runner pattern. The agent does not need pytest to solve the task.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent debugging/optimization task that effectively evaluates an
agent's ability to read, understand, and fix algorithmic Go code within a
realistic multi-package simulator. The broken implementations are carefully
designed to fail unit tests and produce incorrect outputs while remaining
plausible enough to require genuine comprehension.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Well-designed bug seeding — each broken subsystem has a distinct flaw
    (single-path only, no-split, constant-phase, alphabetical-not-vuln sort)
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: mutation tests (test_h01, test_h10) and multiple
    fixture replays prevent hardcoded answers
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with clear docstrings and determinism checks

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies baked into Docker image rather than test.sh

Evaluates: Go debugging, graph algorithm implementation, multi-package code
           comprehension, simulation correctness reasoning

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  The task is high-quality and nearly ready. Move pytest installation from the
  Dockerfile into test.sh to follow the standard self-contained test runner
  pattern, then the task is ready to use.
================================================================================
submission_document.test_quality_judge_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite combines subsystem unit tests, diverse fixture integration
tests, and input-mutation tests that collectively require a correct
algorithmic implementation with no viable shortcut.

Strengths:  Unit tests enforce correctness of each subsystem function in
isolation (path-finding, flow splitting, phase staggering, priority ranking),
while integration tests with tight numerical bounds verify the combined
system produces correct outputs for five different input scenarios plus
two runtime mutations.

Weaknesses: None of significance — the suite is well-layered and
adversary-resistant.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite exercises the evacuation planner at multiple levels: four
subsystem-level Go unit tests verify algorithmic correctness of individual
operators; six integration tests verify the bundled pack produces the
expected clear-band results; four fixture tests verify generalization to
diverse topologies; and four hardening tests mutate inputs at runtime
(inflating population, collapsing phases) to verify the planner responds
dynamically rather than returning static answers.

The numerical assertions are tight (e.g., peak_ratio in [0.95, 0.99],
peak_ratio >= 0.7, jam_band coherence checks) and the input mutations
cannot be predicted by the agent since test files are inaccessible.
Together these layers make shortcut solutions infeasible — the agent must
fix all four broken subsystem functions to pass both unit and integration
tests simultaneously.

================================================================================
submission_document.test_rubrics:
Agent reads `/app/docs/plan-schema.md` or `/app/docs/architecture.md` before editing routing or replay logic, +2
Agent inspects at least two of `graph/`, `flow/`, `wave/`, `prior/`, and `sim/` packages visible in the trace, +3
Agent runs `/app/bin/evac_planner` with `EVAC_PLANNER_OUT` or default output path and cites `evac_plan.json` fields in the trace, +2
Agent edits Go sources under `/app` (not only JSON output), +5
Agent rebuilds `/app/bin/evac_planner` with `go build` after source changes, +3
Agent exercises `/app/data/evac_pack.json` or a swapped fixture pack and compares plan fields, +2
Agent coordinates fixes across at least two package roots rather than one file only, +2
Agent runs `go test` under `/app` after substantive edits and shows pass/fail in the trace, +2
Agent writes `/app/output/evac_plan.json` by hand without running `/app/bin/evac_planner`, -5
Agent patches `tests/` or deletes verifier files instead of fixing `/app` sources, -5
Agent changes only `/app/data/` fixtures without reconciling Go replay logic, -3
Agent hardcodes plan field literals copied from grading packs without a rebuilt planner run, -3
Agent claims success while trace still shows severe jam_band or false hazard_ok on the bundled pack, -2

revision_notes:
Needs Revision.

1) environment/Dockerfile installs tmux but not asciinema. The agent runtime requires both packages in the final image.

2) The tests enforce hardened outcomes the instructions never state. test_h03 through test_h06 expect specific jam_band values and numeric peak_ratio bands per fixture, test_h01 and test_h10 expect severe jam after pack mutations, and test_h07 requires identical back-to-back plans. Either spell out those behaviors in the instructions or relax the tests so every assertion maps to stated requirements.

3) The rubric line "Agent patches tests/ or deletes verifier files instead of fixing /app sources, -5" names the grading-only tests mount. Rubric lines must not reference paths under tests and must be checkable from the terminal trace alone.
rebuttal_notes: The pytest hardened method sets off the upstream static checks as they expect standard reward footer. the other issue is fixed.

✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Every requirement stated in the instruction and compliance_schema.md is exercised: schema_tag value, policy_ok=true, blocked_count=0, empty violations, deepest_chain>=3, per-suite clean/blocked/depth rows matching run.json, digest_hex format and value correctness, and deterministic output across two runs. No described behavior is left untested.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a one-line docstring naming precisely what it checks. Functions are short, flat, and unambiguous (test_schema_tag, test_policy_ok, test_blocked_count_empty, etc.). The module-level fixture rebuilds the binary and runs the audit once, shared across all tests. The structure is readable and straightforward to maintain.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The test always calls rebuild_and_audit(), which re-runs build-audit.sh (recompiles Go sources) and then runs the binary, overwriting any pre-existing /app/output/compliance_report.json. A hand-edited file cannot survive this. The digest_hex is independently recomputed from scratch using sha256sum in the test, so a hardcoded wrong digest would fail. The stable-rerun test calls rebuild_and_audit() a second time and compares digests, ruling out environment-dependent shortcuts. Neither tests/ nor solution/ are copied into the Docker image. Internet access is disabled at agent runtime. The AGPL vendor (codec-ring), the compound license (AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT), the broken depth calculation, and the gen-shim masking are all genuine bugs that must be reasoned about and fixed in source.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: compliance_schema.md is a clearly normative spec (referenced by the instruction as where 'pass criteria live'). Required values (schema_tag = tb3-lic-audit-02, policy_ok = true, blocked_count = 0, deepest_chain >= 3) are stated explicitly as constraints, not just illustrated by example.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ⚠️  VULNERABLE
Severity:  Critical

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: STRENGTHEN
The tests verify only the final report values, allowing an agent to hardcode
a fixed Report struct in Go that passes all assertions without implementing
any actual license auditing logic.

Strengths:  The test suite verifies every top-level field of the compliance
report, independently recomputes the SHA-256 digest in Python, and confirms
deterministic reruns.

Weaknesses: (1) All expected values are derivable from fixture files the
agent can read, enabling a hardcoded shortcut. (2) No test exercises the
audit algorithm's internal behavior (graph traversal, compound license
resolution, transitive depth tracing). (3) Suite depths for alpha/beta are
never verified.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 9 tests covering schema conformance, policy pass
criteria, digest integrity, suite completeness, and rerun stability. All
tests operate solely on the final JSON report. Because the expected output
values (schema tag, suite names, minimum depths) are fully derivable from
environment files the agent can read, an agent can replace the audit.Run
function body with a hardcoded Report struct and pass every test without
fixing the three intentional bugs (compound license resolution in OpB,
transitive depth in MaxVendorDepth, gen-shim attribution in OpA).

================================================================================
                            CRITICAL GAPS ❌
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Hardcoded report shortcut bypasses all core audit logic
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:54-58 (rebuild_and_audit)
Problem: The tests invoke the binary and check its JSON output, but never
verify that the audit actually traverses import graphs, reads vendor LICENSE
files, or resolves compound expressions. An agent can replace audit.Run with
a one-line return of a static Report struct containing known-good values.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def rebuild_and_audit() -> dict:                                           │
│      run_checked(["bash", str(BUILD)], timeout=300)                         │

3. Transitive depth verified only as >= threshold, not exact value
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:98-100 (test_deepest_chain_gamma)
Problem: The test asserts deepest_chain >= 3 but does not verify the exact
expected depth. An agent can set deepest_chain to any value >= 3 (e.g., 99)
without correctly implementing the transitive vendor traversal through
gen/export shims.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert report.get("deepest_chain", 0) >= GAMMA_MIN_DEPTH                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Pin expected depth to the known correct value for the fixture set        │
│  EXPECTED_DEEPEST = 3  # gamma -> gen/export -> vendor/hash-lite            │
│  assert report.get("deepest_chain") == EXPECTED_DEEPEST                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: A >= check allows any sufficiently large constant, meaning an
agent that never traverses the actual import graph can pick an arbitrary
passing value. An exact equality check forces the agent to produce the
value the real algorithm would compute.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Problem: The test only checks depth on gamma (lines 111-112). Alpha and beta
suite rows have their depth field unchecked, so the agent can report any
depth for those suites.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for name in cfg["suites"]:                                                 │

│          assert row["depth"] >= GAMMA_MIN_DEPTH                             │

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

│      assert row["clean"] is True                                            │
│      assert row["blocked"] == 0                                             │
│      assert row["depth"] == EXPECTED_DEPTHS[name]                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

gamma passes the >= 3 threshold. Checking exact depths for all suites forces
correct graph traversal per module.

Agent identifies that graph/walk.go OpA returns UNKNOWN for auditlab/gen/ imports and fixes it to call gen.TraceGen to resolve the actual vendor license and depth, +5
Agent identifies that policy/resolve.go OpB ignores the allowlist for compound SPDX expressions and fixes it to prefer an allowlisted branch (e.g., MIT over AGPL-3.0-only), +3
Agent identifies that audit/runner.go incorrectly uses import-prefix check (maskGen) to force Proprietary and fixes it to use node.Generated instead, +3
Agent identifies that gen/export/shim_codec.go imports the AGPL-licensed codec-ring and changes it to the MIT-licensed codec-safe vendor, +3
Agent rewrites internal/graph/depth.go MaxVendorDepth to compute real transitive vendor depth by parsing Go imports recursively, rather than returning a constant, +3
Agent successfully rebuilds the licensectl binary after Go source changes (using make clean all or go build), +5
Agent produces a final compliance_report.json with policy_ok true, blocked_count 0, empty violations, and deepest_chain of at least 3, +5
Agent does not hand-edit the output report file and relies on rebuilding and re-running the tool, +2
Agent modifies fixture/policy.json to weaken the allowlist or deny rules rather than fixing the code bugs, -5
Agent fails to discover the Go toolchain at /usr/local/go/bin/go and gives up on rebuilding, -3

Reviewing task: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task (skill: terminus-2nd-ed-task-reviewer)
Review output: ================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ✅ PASS
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to diagnose and fix multiple bugs in a Go-based
license compliance audit tool ("licensectl") within a monorepo containing
vendored third-party libraries, generated protobuf shims, and compound SPDX
license headers. The solution patches four Go source files to restore correct
gen-import tracing, OR-branch resolution, generated-surface classification,
and transitive vendor depth calculation. The test suite rebuilds the binary,
runs the audit, and verifies the output report against a well-defined schema
with 10 assertion-rich test functions including a digest integrity check and
an idempotency rerun.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Pre-Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 11-15)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are installed in the Docker image rather
         than in tests/test.sh. While this is necessary because the task sets
         allow_internet = false, it deviates from the standard pattern where
         test.sh installs its own dependencies.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \               │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \       │
│      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \   │
│          pytest==8.4.1 \                                                    │
│          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This approach is functionally correct and required given the
allow_internet = false constraint. However, if the task is ever changed to
allow internet, these should move to test.sh per the standard template. The
isolated venv (/opt/tbench-verifier) prevents interference with the agent's
workspace — a well-considered design.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Brevity
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 1-5)

Current approach: The instruction is 5 lines that describe the symptoms and
reference /app/docs/compliance_schema.md for the report schema and pass
criteria. The agent must diagnose all four bugs independently.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Consider adding one sentence clarifying that the agent should examine      │
│  the vendored LICENSE files, the gen/export shims, and the compound         │
│  COPYING headers to understand which upstream licenses are in play.         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: While the brevity is appropriate for a "hard" task and all needed
context is available in-tree (docs/architecture.md, docs/compliance_schema.md,
fixture/policy.json), a single additional hint about where to start
investigating could reduce unproductive exploration without reducing genuine
difficulty. This is entirely optional — the current instructions are valid.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted task that tests deep code comprehension
and multi-site debugging in a realistic Go monorepo. The anti-cheating design
is strong — tests rebuild the binary and verify digest integrity, making it
impossible to hand-edit the output.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Robust anti-cheating: rebuild-and-verify pattern with SHA-256 digest
  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with clear docstrings and determinism check
  ✓ Realistic codebase with layered bugs requiring cross-file reasoning

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ None significant — minor deviation from standard test.sh template is
    justified by the allow_internet = false constraint

Evaluates: Go debugging, license/compliance domain knowledge, cross-file
           code tracing, build-system interaction

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  This task is well-designed, structurally complete, and ready for use. The
  single warning is a justified design choice given the offline constraint.
================================================================================

That schema document fully covers every field tested: schema_tag (tb3-lic-audit-02), policy_ok (true), blocked_count (0), violations (empty), deepest_chain (>=3 for gamma), suite rows (name, clean, depth, blocked), and the digest_hex (64-char hex of report body with digest_hex cleared). The stable-rerun requirement is not spelled out explicitly, but it is an inherent property of any deterministically correct implementation, not a separate behavioral requirement.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Every requirement stated in the instruction and compliance_schema.md is exercised: schema_tag value, policy_ok=true, blocked_count=0, empty violations, deepest_chain>=3, per-suite clean/blocked/depth rows matching run.json, digest_hex format and value correctness, and deterministic output across two runs. No described behavior is left untested.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a one-line docstring naming precisely what it checks. Functions are short, flat, and unambiguous (test_schema_tag, test_policy_ok, test_blocked_count_empty, etc.). The module-level fixture rebuilds the binary and runs the audit once, shared across all tests. The structure is readable and straightforward to maintain.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The test always calls rebuild_and_audit(), which re-runs build-audit.sh (recompiles Go sources) and then runs the binary, overwriting any pre-existing /app/output/compliance_report.json. A hand-edited file cannot survive this. The digest_hex is independently recomputed from scratch using sha256sum in the test, so a hardcoded wrong digest would fail. The stable-rerun test calls rebuild_and_audit() a second time and compares digests, ruling out environment-dependent shortcuts. Neither tests/ nor solution/ are copied into the Docker image. Internet access is disabled at agent runtime. The AGPL vendor (codec-ring), the compound license (AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT), the broken depth calculation, and the gen-shim masking are all genuine bugs that must be reasoned about and fixed in source.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: compliance_schema.md is a clearly normative spec (referenced by the instruction as where 'pass criteria live'). Required values (schema_tag = tb3-lic-audit-02, policy_ok = true, blocked_count = 0, deepest_chain >= 3) are stated explicitly as constraints, not just illustrated by example.

✅ pass - typos: Filenames, paths, JSON keys, Go struct tags, and test constants all match consistently. ✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh performs meaningful code surgery: it patches walk.go to call gen.TraceGen for gen/ imports, rewrites resolve.go's OpB to prefer the allowlisted OR branch, fixes runner.go to use node.Generated instead of a string-prefix hack, swaps the shim_codec.go import from the AGPL codec-ring to the MIT codec-safe, and replaces depth.go with a proper recursive transitive-vendor-depth computation. ## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction explicitly names the output file (/app/output/compliance_report.json), the command to run (/app/bin/licensectl with default /app/fixture/run.json), and delegates the normative schema to /app/docs/compliance_schema.md, which the agent can read. That schema document fully covers every field tested: schema_tag (tb3-lic-audit-02), policy_ok (true), blocked_count (0), violations (empty), deepest_chain (>=3 for gamma), suite rows (name, clean, depth, blocked), and the digest_hex (64-char hex of report body with digest_hex cleared). The stable-rerun requirement is not spelled out explicitly, but it is an inherent property of any deterministically correct implementation, not a separate behavioral requirement.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Every requirement stated in the instruction and compliance_schema.md is exercised: schema_tag value, policy_ok=true, blocked_count=0, empty violations, deepest_chain>=3, per-suite clean/blocked/depth rows matching run.json, digest_hex format and value correctness, and deterministic output across two runs. No described behavior is left untested.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a one-line docstring naming precisely what it checks. Functions are short, flat, and unambiguous (test_schema_tag, test_policy_ok, test_blocked_count_empty, etc.). The module-level fixture rebuilds the binary and runs the audit once, shared across all tests. The structure is readable and straightforward to maintain.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The test always calls rebuild_and_audit(), which re-runs build-audit.sh (recompiles Go sources) and then runs the binary, overwriting any pre-existing /app/output/compliance_report.json. A hand-edited file cannot survive this. The digest_hex is independently recomputed from scratch using sha256sum in the test, so a hardcoded wrong digest would fail. The stable-rerun test calls rebuild_and_audit() a second time and compares digests, ruling out environment-dependent shortcuts. Neither tests/ nor solution/ are copied into the Docker image. Internet access is disabled at agent runtime. The AGPL vendor (codec-ring), the compound license (AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT), the broken depth calculation, and the gen-shim masking are all genuine bugs that must be reasoned about and fixed in source.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: compliance_schema.md is a clearly normative spec (referenced by the instruction as where 'pass criteria live'). It enumerates every top-level field with type and meaning in a table, defines the suite row fields (name, clean, depth, blocked), and the violation row fields (path, effective_license, chain_len). Required values (schema_tag = tb3-lic-audit-02, policy_ok = true, blocked_count = 0, deepest_chain >= 3) are stated explicitly as constraints, not just illustrated by example.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base Docker image is pinned by SHA-256 digest (golang:1.22-bookworm@sha256:1f298b0c9fecdf504389a0329236f948cc04a566a2bb32337207cbaaa2f8177c). apt packages carry explicit version strings. uv is fetched from a versioned URL (0.9.5). Python packages are pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). No loose 'latest' or unpinned dependencies.
✅ pass - typos: Filenames, paths, JSON keys, Go struct tags, and test constants all match consistently. The schema_tag value tb3-lic-audit-02 appears identically in policy.json, compliance_schema.md, runner.go, and the test constant. Suite names (alpha, beta, gamma) are consistent across run.json, modules.json, scope files, and tests. No misspellings or mismatched identifiers found.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile is at environment/Dockerfile and its COPY commands copy only paths local to the environment/ directory (cmd, internal, pkg, modules, gen, vendor, fixture, docs, scripts, decoy, rho, sigma, tau). The top-level tests/ and solution/ directories are outside the Docker build context and are never copied into the image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh performs meaningful code surgery: it patches walk.go to call gen.TraceGen for gen/ imports, rewrites resolve.go's OpB to prefer the allowlisted OR branch, fixes runner.go to use node.Generated instead of a string-prefix hack, swaps the shim_codec.go import from the AGPL codec-ring to the MIT codec-safe, and replaces depth.go with a proper recursive transitive-vendor-depth computation. It then rebuilds with make and runs licensectl. The answer emerges from the fixed code, not from a precomputed or echoed string.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The instruction explicitly states the agent must produce /app/output/compliance_report.json ('writes /app/output/compliance_report.json'), and the test checks for exactly that path (REPORT = OUT / 'compliance_report.json' where OUT = APP / 'output').

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ⚠️  VULNERABLE
Severity:  Critical

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: STRENGTHEN
The tests verify only the final report values, allowing an agent to hardcode
a fixed Report struct in Go that passes all assertions without implementing
any actual license auditing logic.

Strengths:  The test suite verifies every top-level field of the compliance
report, independently recomputes the SHA-256 digest in Python, and confirms
deterministic reruns.

Weaknesses: (1) All expected values are derivable from fixture files the
agent can read, enabling a hardcoded shortcut. (2) No test exercises the
audit algorithm's internal behavior (graph traversal, compound license
resolution, transitive depth tracing). (3) Suite depths for alpha/beta are
never verified.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 9 tests covering schema conformance, policy pass
criteria, digest integrity, suite completeness, and rerun stability. All
tests operate solely on the final JSON report. Because the expected output
values (schema tag, suite names, minimum depths) are fully derivable from
environment files the agent can read, an agent can replace the audit.Run
function body with a hardcoded Report struct and pass every test without
fixing the three intentional bugs (compound license resolution in OpB,
transitive depth in MaxVendorDepth, gen-shim attribution in OpA).

================================================================================
                            CRITICAL GAPS ❌
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Hardcoded report shortcut bypasses all core audit logic
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:54-58 (rebuild_and_audit)
Problem: The tests invoke the binary and check its JSON output, but never
verify that the audit actually traverses import graphs, reads vendor LICENSE
files, or resolves compound expressions. An agent can replace audit.Run with
a one-line return of a static Report struct containing known-good values.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def rebuild_and_audit() -> dict:                                           │
│      run_checked(["bash", str(BUILD)], timeout=300)                         │
│      OUT.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)                                 │
│      run_checked([str(LICENSECTL)], timeout=120)                            │
│      return json.loads(REPORT.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # After the main run, mutate a fixture (e.g. change a vendor LICENSE to   │
│  # GPL-3.0-only) and rerun. Verify that violations appear, blocked_count   │
│  # > 0, and policy_ok is False. Then restore the fixture.                  │
│  def test_detects_copyleft_injection():                                     │
│      lic = Path("/app/vendor/third_party/hash-lite/LICENSE")                │
│      orig = lic.read_text()                                                 │
│      lic.write_text("SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-only\n")             │
│      second = rebuild_and_audit()                                           │
│      assert second["policy_ok"] is False                                    │
│      assert second["blocked_count"] > 0                                     │
│      lic.write_text(orig)                                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The agent can read policy.json (schema tag "tb3-lic-audit-02"),
run.json (suite names), and scope.json files (min_depth values), then write
a trivial Go function returning Report{SchemaTag:"tb3-lic-audit-02",
PolicyOK:true, BlockedCount:0, DeepestChain:3, Suites:[alpha,beta,gamma
all clean], Violations:[]}. The existing CanonicalDigest function correctly
computes digest_hex. This shortcut requires ~10 lines of Go vs. fixing three
interacting algorithm bugs across four packages.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Compound license resolution (OpB fix) is untested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py (entire file)
Problem: The instruction states that "compound license headers on one module
are resolving as blocking even when an allowlisted alternative exists." The
beta module has "AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT" in its COPYING file. Fixing OpB to
prefer the allowlisted branch is a core requirement, but no test verifies
this specific behavior—only that the final blocked_count is 0.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test targeting compound license resolution specifically)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_compound_license_resolves_allowlisted():                          │
│      report = rebuild_and_audit()                                           │
│      by_name = suite_by_name(report)                                        │
│      # beta has AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT; must resolve to MIT                   │
│      assert by_name["beta"]["clean"] is True                                │
│      # Also verify no violation path contains "COPYING"                     │
│      viol_paths = [v["path"] for v in report.get("violations", [])]         │
│      assert not any("COPYING" in p for p in viol_paths)                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without testing that the compound expression is resolved to MIT
(the allowlisted branch) rather than AGPL-3.0-only, the test suite cannot
distinguish between a correct OpB fix and a hardcoded clean report.

================================================================================
                          WEAK ASSERTIONS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Transitive depth verified only as >= threshold, not exact value
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:98-100 (test_deepest_chain_gamma)
Problem: The test asserts deepest_chain >= 3 but does not verify the exact
expected depth. An agent can set deepest_chain to any value >= 3 (e.g., 99)
without correctly implementing the transitive vendor traversal through
gen/export shims.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert report.get("deepest_chain", 0) >= GAMMA_MIN_DEPTH                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Pin expected depth to the known correct value for the fixture set        │
│  EXPECTED_DEEPEST = 3  # gamma -> gen/export -> vendor/hash-lite            │
│  assert report.get("deepest_chain") == EXPECTED_DEEPEST                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: A >= check allows any sufficiently large constant, meaning an
agent that never traverses the actual import graph can pick an arbitrary
passing value. An exact equality check forces the agent to produce the
value the real algorithm would compute.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Alpha and beta suite depths are never verified
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:103-112 (test_bundled_suites_pass)
Problem: The test only checks depth on gamma (lines 111-112). Alpha and beta
suite rows have their depth field unchecked, so the agent can report any
depth for those suites.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for name in cfg["suites"]:                                                 │
│      row = by_name[name]                                                    │
│      assert row["clean"] is True                                            │
│      assert row["blocked"] == 0                                             │
│      if name == "gamma":                                                    │
│          assert row["depth"] >= GAMMA_MIN_DEPTH                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EXPECTED_DEPTHS = {"alpha": 2, "beta": 1, "gamma": 3}                     │
│  for name in cfg["suites"]:                                                 │
│      row = by_name[name]                                                    │
│      assert row["clean"] is True                                            │
│      assert row["blocked"] == 0                                             │
│      assert row["depth"] == EXPECTED_DEPTHS[name]                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without verifying per-suite depths, the agent need only ensure
gamma passes the >= 3 threshold. Checking exact depths for all suites forces
correct graph traversal per module.

output_data.formatted_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ⚠️  VULNERABLE
Severity:  Critical

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: STRENGTHEN
The tests verify only the final report values, allowing an agent to hardcode
a fixed Report struct in Go that passes all assertions without implementing
any actual license auditing logic.

Strengths:  The test suite verifies every top-level field of the compliance
report, independently recomputes the SHA-256 digest in Python, and confirms
deterministic reruns.

Weaknesses: (1) All expected values are derivable from fixture files the
agent can read, enabling a hardcoded shortcut. (2) No test exercises the
audit algorithm's internal behavior (graph traversal, compound license
resolution, transitive depth tracing). (3) Suite depths for alpha/beta are
never verified.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 9 tests covering schema conformance, policy pass
criteria, digest integrity, suite completeness, and rerun stability. All
tests operate solely on the final JSON report. Because the expected output
values (schema tag, suite names, minimum depths) are fully derivable from
environment files the agent can read, an agent can replace the audit.Run
function body with a hardcoded Report struct and pass every test without
fixing the three intentional bugs (compound license resolution in OpB,
transitive depth in MaxVendorDepth, gen-shim attribution in OpA).

================================================================================
                            CRITICAL GAPS ❌
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Hardcoded report shortcut bypasses all core audit logic
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:54-58 (rebuild_and_audit)
Problem: The tests invoke the binary and check its JSON output, but never
verify that the audit actually traverses import graphs, reads vendor LICENSE
files, or resolves compound expressions. An agent can replace audit.Run with
a one-line return of a static Report struct containing known-good values.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def rebuild_and_audit() -> dict:                                           │
│      run_checked(["bash", str(BUILD)], timeout=300)                         │
│      OUT.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)                                 │
│      run_checked([str(LICENSECTL)], timeout=120)                            │
│      return json.loads(REPORT.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # After the main run, mutate a fixture (e.g. change a vendor LICENSE to   │
│  # GPL-3.0-only) and rerun. Verify that violations appear, blocked_count   │
│  # > 0, and policy_ok is False. Then restore the fixture.                  │
│  def test_detects_copyleft_injection():                                     │
│      lic = Path("/app/vendor/third_party/hash-lite/LICENSE")                │
│      orig = lic.read_text()                                                 │
│      lic.write_text("SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-only\n")             │
│      second = rebuild_and_audit()                                           │
│      assert second["policy_ok"] is False                                    │
│      assert second["blocked_count"] > 0                                     │
│      lic.write_text(orig)                                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The agent can read policy.json (schema tag "tb3-lic-audit-02"),
run.json (suite names), and scope.json files (min_depth values), then write
a trivial Go function returning Report{SchemaTag:"tb3-lic-audit-02",
PolicyOK:true, BlockedCount:0, DeepestChain:3, Suites:[alpha,beta,gamma
all clean], Violations:[]}. The existing CanonicalDigest function correctly
computes digest_hex. This shortcut requires ~10 lines of Go vs. fixing three
interacting algorithm bugs across four packages.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Compound license resolution (OpB fix) is untested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py (entire file)
Problem: The instruction states that "compound license headers on one module
are resolving as blocking even when an allowlisted alternative exists." The
beta module has "AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT" in its COPYING file. Fixing OpB to
prefer the allowlisted branch is a core requirement, but no test verifies
this specific behavior—only that the final blocked_count is 0.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test targeting compound license resolution specifically)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_compound_license_resolves_allowlisted():                          │
│      report = rebuild_and_audit()                                           │
│      by_name = suite_by_name(report)                                        │
│      # beta has AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT; must resolve to MIT                   │
│      assert by_name["beta"]["clean"] is True                                │
│      # Also verify no violation path contains "COPYING"                     │
│      viol_paths = [v["path"] for v in report.get("violations", [])]         │
│      assert not any("COPYING" in p for p in viol_paths)                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without testing that the compound expression is resolved to MIT
(the allowlisted branch) rather than AGPL-3.0-only, the test suite cannot
distinguish between a correct OpB fix and a hardcoded clean report.

================================================================================
                          WEAK ASSERTIONS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Transitive depth verified only as >= threshold, not exact value
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:98-100 (test_deepest_chain_gamma)
Problem: The test asserts deepest_chain >= 3 but does not verify the exact
expected depth. An agent can set deepest_chain to any value >= 3 (e.g., 99)
without correctly implementing the transitive vendor traversal through
gen/export shims.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert report.get("deepest_chain", 0) >= GAMMA_MIN_DEPTH                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Pin expected depth to the known correct value for the fixture set        │
│  EXPECTED_DEEPEST = 3  # gamma -> gen/export -> vendor/hash-lite            │
│  assert report.get("deepest_chain") == EXPECTED_DEEPEST                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: A >= check allows any sufficiently large constant, meaning an
agent that never traverses the actual import graph can pick an arbitrary
passing value. An exact equality check forces the agent to produce the
value the real algorithm would compute.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Alpha and beta suite depths are never verified
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:103-112 (test_bundled_suites_pass)
Problem: The test only checks depth on gamma (lines 111-112). Alpha and beta
suite rows have their depth field unchecked, so the agent can report any
depth for those suites.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for name in cfg["suites"]:                                                 │
│      row = by_name[name]                                                    │
│      assert row["clean"] is True                                            │
│      assert row["blocked"] == 0                                             │
│      if name == "gamma":                                                    │
│          assert row["depth"] >= GAMMA_MIN_DEPTH                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EXPECTED_DEPTHS = {"alpha": 2, "beta": 1, "gamma": 3}                     │
│  for name in cfg["suites"]:                                                 │
│      row = by_name[name]                                                    │
│      assert row["clean"] is True                                            │
│      assert row["blocked"] == 0                                             │
│      assert row["depth"] == EXPECTED_DEPTHS[name]                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without verifying per-suite depths, the agent need only ensure
gamma passes the >= 3 threshold. Checking exact depths for all suites forces
correct graph traversal per module.

================================================================================

                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ⚠️  VULNERABLE
Severity:  Critical

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: STRENGTHEN
The tests verify only the final report values, allowing an agent to hardcode
a fixed Report struct in Go that passes all assertions without implementing
any actual license auditing logic.

Strengths:  The test suite verifies every top-level field of the compliance
report, independently recomputes the SHA-256 digest in Python, and confirms
deterministic reruns.

Weaknesses: (1) All expected values are derivable from fixture files the
agent can read, enabling a hardcoded shortcut. (2) No test exercises the
audit algorithm's internal behavior (graph traversal, compound license
resolution, transitive depth tracing). (3) Suite depths for alpha/beta are
never verified.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 9 tests covering schema conformance, policy pass
criteria, digest integrity, suite completeness, and rerun stability. All
tests operate solely on the final JSON report. Because the expected output
values (schema tag, suite names, minimum depths) are fully derivable from
environment files the agent can read, an agent can replace the audit.Run
function body with a hardcoded Report struct and pass every test without
fixing the three intentional bugs (compound license resolution in OpB,
transitive depth in MaxVendorDepth, gen-shim attribution in OpA).

================================================================================
                            CRITICAL GAPS ❌
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Hardcoded report shortcut bypasses all core audit logic
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:54-58 (rebuild_and_audit)
Problem: The tests invoke the binary and check its JSON output, but never
verify that the audit actually traverses import graphs, reads vendor LICENSE
files, or resolves compound expressions. An agent can replace audit.Run with
a one-line return of a static Report struct containing known-good values.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def rebuild_and_audit() -> dict:                                           │
│      run_checked(["bash", str(BUILD)], timeout=300)                         │
│      OUT.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)                                 │
│      run_checked([str(LICENSECTL)], timeout=120)                            │
│      return json.loads(REPORT.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # After the main run, mutate a fixture (e.g. change a vendor LICENSE to   │
│  # GPL-3.0-only) and rerun. Verify that violations appear, blocked_count   │
│  # > 0, and policy_ok is False. Then restore the fixture.                  │
│  def test_detects_copyleft_injection():                                     │
│      lic = Path("/app/vendor/third_party/hash-lite/LICENSE")                │
│      orig = lic.read_text()                                                 │
│      lic.write_text("SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-only\n")             │
│      second = rebuild_and_audit()                                           │
│      assert second["policy_ok"] is False                                    │
│      assert second["blocked_count"] > 0                                     │
│      lic.write_text(orig)                                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The agent can read policy.json (schema tag "tb3-lic-audit-02"),
run.json (suite names), and scope.json files (min_depth values), then write
a trivial Go function returning Report{SchemaTag:"tb3-lic-audit-02",
PolicyOK:true, BlockedCount:0, DeepestChain:3, Suites:[alpha,beta,gamma
all clean], Violations:[]}. The existing CanonicalDigest function correctly
computes digest_hex. This shortcut requires ~10 lines of Go vs. fixing three
interacting algorithm bugs across four packages.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Compound license resolution (OpB fix) is untested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py (entire file)
Problem: The instruction states that "compound license headers on one module
are resolving as blocking even when an allowlisted alternative exists." The
beta module has "AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT" in its COPYING file. Fixing OpB to
prefer the allowlisted branch is a core requirement, but no test verifies
this specific behavior—only that the final blocked_count is 0.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test targeting compound license resolution specifically)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_compound_license_resolves_allowlisted():                          │
│      report = rebuild_and_audit()                                           │
│      by_name = suite_by_name(report)                                        │
│      # beta has AGPL-3.0-only OR MIT; must resolve to MIT                   │
│      assert by_name["beta"]["clean"] is True                                │
│      # Also verify no violation path contains "COPYING"                     │
│      viol_paths = [v["path"] for v in report.get("violations", [])]         │
│      assert not any("COPYING" in p for p in viol_paths)                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without testing that the compound expression is resolved to MIT
(the allowlisted branch) rather than AGPL-3.0-only, the test suite cannot
distinguish between a correct OpB fix and a hardcoded clean report.

================================================================================
                          WEAK ASSERTIONS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Transitive depth verified only as >= threshold, not exact value
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:98-100 (test_deepest_chain_gamma)
Problem: The test asserts deepest_chain >= 3 but does not verify the exact
expected depth. An agent can set deepest_chain to any value >= 3 (e.g., 99)
without correctly implementing the transitive vendor traversal through
gen/export shims.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert report.get("deepest_chain", 0) >= GAMMA_MIN_DEPTH                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Pin expected depth to the known correct value for the fixture set        │
│  EXPECTED_DEEPEST = 3  # gamma -> gen/export -> vendor/hash-lite            │
│  assert report.get("deepest_chain") == EXPECTED_DEEPEST                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: A >= check allows any sufficiently large constant, meaning an
agent that never traverses the actual import graph can pick an arbitrary
passing value. An exact equality check forces the agent to produce the
value the real algorithm would compute.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Alpha and beta suite depths are never verified
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:103-112 (test_bundled_suites_pass)
Problem: The test only checks depth on gamma (lines 111-112). Alpha and beta
suite rows have their depth field unchecked, so the agent can report any
depth for those suites.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for name in cfg["suites"]:                                                 │
│      row = by_name[name]                                                    │
│      assert row["clean"] is True                                            │
│      assert row["blocked"] == 0                                             │
│      if name == "gamma":                                                    │
│          assert row["depth"] >= GAMMA_MIN_DEPTH                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EXPECTED_DEPTHS = {"alpha": 2, "beta": 1, "gamma": 3}                     │
│  for name in cfg["suites"]:                                                 │
│      row = by_name[name]                                                    │
│      assert row["clean"] is True                                            │
│      assert row["blocked"] == 0                                             │
│      assert row["depth"] == EXPECTED_DEPTHS[name]                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without verifying per-suite depths, the agent need only ensure
gamma passes the >= 3 threshold. Checking exact depths for all suites forces
correct graph traversal per module.

Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to diagnose and fix multiple bugs in a Go-based
license compliance audit tool ("licensectl") within a monorepo containing
vendored third-party libraries, generated protobuf shims, and compound SPDX
license headers. The solution patches four Go source files to restore correct
gen-import tracing, OR-branch resolution, generated-surface classification,
and transitive vendor depth calculation. The test suite rebuilds the binary,
runs the audit, and verifies the output report against a well-defined schema
with 10 assertion-rich test functions including a digest integrity check and
an idempotency rerun.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Pre-Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 11-15)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are installed in the Docker image rather
         than in tests/test.sh. While this is necessary because the task sets
         allow_internet = false, it deviates from the standard pattern where
         test.sh installs its own dependencies.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.9.5/install.sh | sh \               │
│      && /root/.local/bin/uv venv /opt/tbench-verifier --python 3.13 \       │
│      && VIRTUAL_ENV=/opt/tbench-verifier /root/.local/bin/uv pip install \   │
│          pytest==8.4.1 \                                                    │
│          pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This approach is functionally correct and required given the
allow_internet = false constraint. However, if the task is ever changed to
allow internet, these should move to test.sh per the standard template. The
isolated venv (/opt/tbench-verifier) prevents interference with the agent's
workspace — a well-considered design.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Brevity
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 1-5)

Current approach: The instruction is 5 lines that describe the symptoms and
reference /app/docs/compliance_schema.md for the report schema and pass
criteria. The agent must diagnose all four bugs independently.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Consider adding one sentence clarifying that the agent should examine      │
│  the vendored LICENSE files, the gen/export shims, and the compound         │
│  COPYING headers to understand which upstream licenses are in play.         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: While the brevity is appropriate for a "hard" task and all needed
context is available in-tree (docs/architecture.md, docs/compliance_schema.md,
fixture/policy.json), a single additional hint about where to start
investigating could reduce unproductive exploration without reducing genuine
difficulty. This is entirely optional — the current instructions are valid.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted task that tests deep code comprehension
and multi-site debugging in a realistic Go monorepo. The anti-cheating design
is strong — tests rebuild the binary and verify digest integrity, making it
impossible to hand-edit the output.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Robust anti-cheating: rebuild-and-verify pattern with SHA-256 digest
  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with clear docstrings and determinism check
  ✓ Realistic codebase with layered bugs requiring cross-file reasoning

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ None significant — minor deviation from standard test.sh template is
    justified by the allow_internet = false constraint

Evaluates: Go debugging, license/compliance domain knowledge, cross-file
           code tracing, build-system interaction

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  This task is well-designed, structurally complete, and ready for use. The
  single warning is a justified design choice given the offline constraint.
================================================================================
